worth_it_explanation: Every impression Equativ records but the publisher's own ad server does not is revenue the publisher may be billing against numbers that will not survive reconciliation with a buyer. A discrepancy that no one measures cannot be argued down, so it erodes trust and, at month end, makes the invoice harder to defend. This check is a read of counts the publisher already has in two systems, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete counting or timeout difference the publisher can act on.
intro: Equativ (formerly Smart AdServer) and a publisher's own ad server rarely report the exact same impression count for the same inventory, and a gap between them is expected. The questions this subcategory asks are whether the publisher measures that gap against a tolerance they have agreed on, and whether they investigate the sources of the gap when it runs wide. These checks treat the discrepancy as a number to be watched and explained rather than a surprise that surfaces only when a buyer disputes an invoice.

audit_checks.title: Impression discrepancy between Equativ and the ad server is not measured against a tolerance
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A discrepancy that no one tracks cannot be caught when it widens. Without an agreed tolerance and a regular comparison, a publisher learns that Equativ and the ad server disagree only when a buyer disputes a count, by which point the billing period has closed and the difference is harder to reconcile.

educate.base:
Two systems count the same impression at different moments, so they rarely agree to the exact number. Equativ (formerly Smart AdServer) records an impression at one point in the delivery sequence, and a publisher's own ad server records it at another. The Media Rating Council (MRC) and IAB Tech Lab guidelines push impression counting toward a 'count-on-begin-to-render' point measured client-side, as close to final delivery as the system can get, which is why a server-side count taken earlier in the chain will read higher than a client-side count taken later. A gap between Equativ and the ad server is the expected result of that timing difference, not a failure on its own.

The problem is not that the two numbers differ. The problem is not knowing how far apart they are or whether today's gap is normal. A discrepancy of a few percent may be routine for a given placement, while the same few percent on a different unit may signal a counting or delivery fault. Without a number the publisher has agreed to treat as the ceiling, every gap looks either fine or alarming with nothing to judge it against, and a widening trend slips by unnoticed.

Measuring the discrepancy means comparing Equativ's impression count against the ad server's count for matched inventory over a defined window, expressing the gap as a percentage, and holding it against a tolerance the publisher has set. Industry practice often treats a single-digit percentage as routine for display, but the right ceiling depends on the placement, the format, and what buyers have agreed to, so the tolerance is a decision the publisher records rather than a fixed industry number.

A few signs point to a discrepancy that is going unmeasured:
  • No regular report compares Equativ's impression count against the ad server's count for the same inventory
  • The publisher cannot state the typical gap for a given placement or the tolerance above which it warrants investigation
  • Discrepancies surface only when a buyer or partner disputes a count rather than from the publisher's own monitoring
You may want to confirm that the impression gap between Equativ and the ad server is measured on a regular cadence and held against a tolerance the publisher has agreed on. One approach is to build a recurring comparison by placement and treat any gap beyond the tolerance as a flag for the second check in this subcategory.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, premium direct-sold placements are often billed against the publisher's own ad server count while programmatic demand through Equativ counts separately, so a gap between the two reads straight into invoices a buyer can dispute. You may want to measure the discrepancy on the billed placements first and set a tighter tolerance there than on remnant inventory.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, sponsored product placements and editorial inventory can clear through different paths and carry different typical gaps, so a single blended discrepancy number can hide a wide gap on the commercial units. You may want to measure the discrepancy per inventory type rather than as one site-wide figure.

learn_more.source_title: MRC, IAB Tech Lab, and MMA Announce Revised Ad Measurement Guidelines (count-on-begin-to-render)

educate.learn_more_note: Equativ's detailed reporting and reconciliation documentation for matching its counts against an external ad server is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review whether a regular comparison exists between Equativ's impression count and the ad server's count for matched inventory, and whether the publisher holds the gap against a stated tolerance.

equativ_reporting.label: Equativ reporting
steps: Open Equativ's reporting and pull impression counts for a defined window, broken out by the inventory or ad units that also run through the publisher's own ad server.
Record the impression total per placement and the date range so it can be matched against the ad server figures for the same period.
Ask the publisher whether this comparison is run on a schedule and whether a tolerance for the gap has been agreed, and record the answer.

ad_server_reporting.label: Publisher ad server reporting
steps: Pull the ad server's impression count for the same inventory and the same window used in the Equativ report.
Calculate the gap per placement as a percentage of the ad server count, so the discrepancy is expressed in comparable terms.
Note whether any placement's gap exceeds the publisher's stated tolerance, or whether no tolerance exists to judge it against.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per matched placement, Equativ's impression count, the ad server's count, the gap as a percentage, and the tolerance the publisher has agreed on. A setup where the discrepancy is measured on a regular cadence and held against a stated tolerance passes. A setup where no regular comparison exists, or where the publisher cannot state a tolerance to judge the gap against, is a fail recorded with the placements affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record Equativ's impression count, the ad server's count, the percentage gap, and the agreed tolerance per placement above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not measure the impression discrepancy between Equativ and its own ad server against an agreed tolerance, so the gap between the two counts goes unwatched. Equativ (formerly Smart AdServer) and the ad server count an impression at different points in delivery, so a gap is expected, but without a tolerance to judge it against a widening trend passes unnoticed until a buyer disputes a count. {{voice_recommend}} building a recurring comparison of Equativ's impression count against the ad server's count for matched inventory, expressing the gap as a percentage, and setting a tolerance per placement above which the gap warrants investigation. After the comparison is in place, treat any gap beyond the tolerance as a flag to investigate its source.

audit_checks.title: Discrepancy sources are not investigated when the gap runs beyond tolerance
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Measuring a gap without diagnosing it leaves the publisher knowing a problem exists but not what is causing it. A wide discrepancy can come from a timeout, added latency, a different counting point, or a blocked creative, and each has a different fix, so a gap left undiagnosed is revenue or trust lost to a cause the publisher could have corrected.

educate.base:
When the gap between Equativ and the ad server runs beyond the tolerance, the number says a problem exists but not why. A wide discrepancy has more than one possible source, and the right correction depends on which one is at work, so a gap that gets measured but never traced back to a cause leaves the publisher stuck at the symptom.

The common sources fall into a short list a practitioner can work through. Several causes account for most wide gaps:
  • Timeouts: a bid or a measurement call that arrives after the window closes does not reach or register in the system waiting on it, so one side counts the impression and the other does not
  • Latency: slow page or creative load delays the rendering point, so a client-side count taken at render falls short of a server-side count taken earlier
  • Counting method: one system counts when the ad is decided or served while the other counts when it begins to render, which the MRC and IAB Tech Lab guidelines place later in the chain, so the earlier count reads higher
  • Blocked creatives: an ad blocker or a creative that fails to render is counted by the server that sent it but never by the client that was meant to display it
In a header-bidding setup, timeouts are a frequent source because a bid response that comes back after the configured timeout is not sent on to the ad server, as the Prebid documentation describes, so the demand path and the ad server can disagree on what was delivered. Equativ is compatible with Prebid, so the same timeout mechanics apply where header bidding feeds it.

Tracing a discrepancy means matching impressions across the systems rather than comparing two totals. Shared identifiers carried on the impression let a publisher line up the same event in each system and see where it was counted and where it was not, which turns a percentage gap into a list of specific impressions and the point at which they diverged. Prebid's reconciliation tooling exists for this purpose, collecting vendor-specific impression IDs so records can be matched across the ad server, the supply-side platforms, and verification systems.

You may want to confirm that a gap beyond tolerance triggers an investigation into its source rather than being logged and left. One approach is to work through the timeout, latency, counting-method, and blocked-creative causes in turn, using matched impression identifiers to isolate which one the gap traces to before deciding on a fix.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, heavy article pages with multiple ad slots and rich media can add the latency that delays a render-time count, so a wide gap on those pages may trace to load time rather than counting. You may want to check page and creative load on the high-discrepancy placements before assuming a counting difference.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product pages often carry third-party tags and personalization scripts that compete for load time and can push the rendering point later, widening a client-side count's shortfall. You may want to investigate latency on the commercial pages specifically when their discrepancy runs beyond tolerance.

learn_more.source_title: Reconciliation Real Time Data Provider module

educate.learn_more_note: Equativ's own configuration documentation for its ad server timeout and counting settings is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: For any placement whose gap runs beyond tolerance, work through the likely sources, timeouts, latency, counting method, and blocked creatives, using matched impression identifiers to isolate which one the gap traces to.

equativ_reporting.label: Equativ reporting
steps: For a placement flagged in the first check, pull Equativ's impression and request data for the window and note where in delivery Equativ records the impression.
Check whether the placement runs header bidding through Prebid into Equativ, and if so record the configured timeout since responses arriving after it are dropped before the ad server.
Where impression identifiers are available, capture them so the same impressions can be matched against the ad server's records.

ad_server_reporting.label: Publisher ad server reporting
steps: Pull the ad server's count for the same placement and window and note the point at which it counts the impression, for example begin-to-render versus served.
Match impressions across the two systems using the shared identifiers, and isolate the events one system counted and the other did not.

For the unmatched events, work through the candidate causes: a timeout dropping late responses, latency delaying the render-time count, a counting-method difference, or creatives blocked before they rendered. Record which cause the evidence points to.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each placement whose gap exceeded the tolerance, whether the discrepancy was traced to a source and which one, for example a timeout, added latency, a counting-method difference, or blocked creatives. A setup where out-of-tolerance gaps are investigated to a cause and the cause is recorded passes. A setup where a gap beyond tolerance is logged but left undiagnosed is a fail recorded with the placements and gaps involved.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record, per out-of-tolerance placement, whether the gap was traced to a source and which cause it pointed to above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} measures the impression gap between Equativ and the ad server but does not investigate the source when the gap runs beyond tolerance, so the cause stays unknown and uncorrected. A wide discrepancy can come from a timeout dropping late bid responses, added latency delaying a render-time count, a counting-method difference between the two systems, or creatives blocked before they rendered, and each carries a different fix. {{voice_recommend}} working through those candidate causes for each out-of-tolerance placement, using shared impression identifiers to match the same events across Equativ and the ad server and isolate where they diverged. After the source is identified, apply the fix that matches the cause rather than treating every gap the same way.
